data.title: Traditional Chinese medicine gets a delicious, Insta-worthy makeover in food and drink
data.description:
The food and drink world is experiencing the rise of a secret ingredient straight out of the history books: traditional Chinese medicine (TCM).
For centuries across Chinese cultures, TCM has emphasised balancing the body’s qi, its vital life and energy source. One of the easiest ways to achieve bodily harmony is by treating food as medicine.
Now, these historic medicines are getting a modern, delicious makeover – preventive wellness made easy, tasty and Instagram-worthy – that appeals to a younger generation.
From Shanghai to Kuala Lumpur, here are three gelato and cocktail businesses incorporating TCM into their menus.
1. Que Tang Yu Fang, Kuala Lumpur
Que Tang Yu Fang is a tea shop in the Malaysian capital’s Bukit Jalil neighbourhood that freshly brews TCM-inspired drinks daily, balancing unique flavours with therapeutic efficacy.

When this herbal tea shop launched in September, Asean Records – a body that recognises exceptional achievements and contributions across the Asean region – anointed it as the “first TCM cafe chain to integrate AI health screening for real-time diagnosis and herbal drink recommendations from patented TCM formulas”.
Que Tang Yu Fang’s innovation lies in its use of two tools: AI tongue analysis and AI pulse diagnosis devices. They provide data used in an on-the-spot health assessment to recommend a herbal drink on the menu suited to the customer’s health needs.
Some of these drinks include the Yin-yang Harmoniser, made from mulberry juice and goji berry; the Crimson Comfort, brewed with dried ginger and longan aril; and the Feather Effect, distilled from lotus leaf and dried tangerine peel.

Those who visit will not only leave the store with a tasty drink in hand, but also a digital health report, which can be sent to their phones for future reference.
Lot 2.07.00, Level 2, Pavilion Bukit Jalil, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
2. Zhong Yao Tang, Shanghai
Breaking boundaries in the dessert world, Zhong Yao Tang burst onto the Shanghai scene in 2024 with TCM-infused gelato.
Located in the city’s Xuhui district, which is also home to the Shanghai Botanical Gardens and historic places of worship such as the Buddhist Longhua Temple, this gelato shop transforms traditional herbal remedies – think Pei Pa Koa cough syrup and chrysanthemum flowers – into surprisingly sophisticated and balanced ice cream.

Each scoop is crafted with natural herbs and fruits, with its unique TCM-inspired flavours including pistachio chrysanthemum, bamboo jasmine and white tea singhara nut.
Patrons should be aware that TCM generally discourages cold foods, which are thought to disrupt the balance of qi and weaken the digestive system.
However, Zhong Yao Tang’s menu items are less sweet than typical desserts, making them a pick-me-up treat that you can indulge in while also being mindful of your health.
For those who are not ice cream lovers but would still like to check out this shop, the store also offers herbal jelly, herbal drinks and fruit candy.
267 Xiangyang Nan Lu, Xuhui District, Shanghai, China
3. Herbal Bar, Shanghai
Alcohol is not strictly discouraged in TCM; in fact, medicinal wines are still used as powerful tonics to treat ailments, as alcohol can help extract the functional compounds of herbs.
If you are looking for that perfect weekend buzz but want to nourish your body with enriching ingredients at the same time, Herbal Bar in Shanghai might be your next stop.
Founded by TCM students from Shanghai University, Herbal Bar began in China’s Anhui province and now operates in two different locations in Shanghai.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Tina Xu (@tina.xuuu)



Customers visiting between 8pm and 11pm can receive a consultation from recently graduated TCM doctors and medical students, which involves having their pulse and tongue checked.
Looking at customers’ individual needs, staff curate specific concoctions with a huangjiu base with either heat-clearing, liver-soothing or energy-boosting properties, along with providing personalised lifestyle and dietary recommendations.
Non-alcoholic herbal teas are also available along with low-alcohol cocktails.
Blending herbal wisdom with modern bartending techniques, this Shanghai bar is quickly becoming a gathering place for Chinese youth to explore trendy herbal drinks.
Shop 1603, 16/F, 248 Daxue Road, Shanghai, China and 100 Xiangyang North Road, Shanghai, China
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Cheaper electricity bills are nice but not enough
data.description:
A monthly HK$10 saving may not mean much in a city notorious for its high cost of living. But to many hard-pressed Hong Kong households, a modest 2 per cent cut in electricity tariffs comes as a surprise.
The reduction seems to defy the perception that the saying “what goes up must come down” does not apply to utility bills. But the cut remains the exception rather than the norm, as this is only the second time since 2017 that the annual electricity tariff adjustment has resulted in users paying less.
The truth is that the adjustment is based on a basket of factors that are too complex for public understanding. As the government said, the two power companies eventually agreed to lower the average net tariff “after rounds of negotiations”.
The basic tariffs continue to spiral as a result of rising operation costs, with CLP Power charging 3.3 per cent more to 101.2 HK cents per kilowatt-hour (13 US cents per kWh) and HK Electric raising the rate by 4.1 per cent to 127.9 HK cents per kWh. But thanks to the reduced fuel surcharge, the adjustments for CLP and HK Electric in 2026 will translate into 2.6 and 2.2 per cent cuts respectively, or about HK$10 less per month for a three-member family.
The cheaper bills will provide welcome respite for households and businesses, many of which are still struggling to cope in an unsettling economic environment. The two power giants are also well aware of their social responsibility and are dishing out an array of concessions and green initiatives for low-income groups. More should be done in light of their healthy financial situation.
Inevitably, the transition towards green energy, as well as investment against extreme weather and cyberattacks, will continue to drive up operating costs. This is not helped when a controversial scheme of control guarantees the two companies a return of up to 8 per cent based on the value of their electricity generation assets. The agreement has limited the scope of manoeuvre of the government and put users in a disadvantaged position.
It so happens that lower fuel costs have resulted in a modest cut in energy bills for the coming year. A more reasonable adjustment mechanism remains the way forward in the longer term.

data.title: The rich history of cinnamon, from Ceylon to Cinnabon
data.description:
Pastry chef Steve Hodge likes to make cinnamon toast – a quick snack for his kids at home. He spreads room-temperature butter on freshly toasted bread and then sprinkles a mixture of equal parts cinnamon and sugar on top.
“When it’s nice and warm, the sugar kind of melts and infuses with the cinnamon. The flavour is unreal,” observes Hodge, the owner of Vancouver-based Temper Chocolate & Pastry.
For many, the smell of cinnamon evokes the flavours of autumn and winter, including apple pie, pumpkin pie, cinnamon rolls, eggnog and mulled wine. Hodge says that’s because the woody spice has a warmth to it.

“Cinnamon has a lot of great flavours because there’s an earthy, woody taste to it,” he says. “If you were to smell or chew on a cinnamon stick, there is a spiced note to it.” He adds that, depending on what ingredients you cook with, cinnamon can enhance the flavours of other spices, contribute a subtle sweetness to spicy food and even provide a hint of acidity.
This woody spice comes from the inner bark of trees in the genus Cinnamomum. “True cinnamon” (Cinnamomum verum), or Ceylon cinnamon, comes from Sri Lanka, formerly known as Ceylon. However, most of the world’s cinnamon production – about 90 per cent – comes from related species grown in Indonesia, Vietnam and China. Ceylon cinnamon has a lighter, milder and sweeter flavour compared to the stronger, spicier and more pungent taste of other varieties.

While cinnamon is commonly used in baking, it is also found in savoury dishes like soups and curries. The spice is added to everything from breakfast cereals to hot chocolate and ice cream. Studies exploring cinnamon’s health benefits suggest it may help support healthy blood pressure, blood sugar and metabolism, and reduce inflammation.
Here in Asia, the spice is synonymous with Chinese five-spice powder, fiery Indonesian and Malaysian rendang, and the festive Korean punch known as sujeonggwa. In the West, of course, cinnamon is known for its association with apple pies. When Hodge makes them, he uses Granny Smiths, explaining that the spice balances the apples’ sharp acidity.
When combining cinnamon and chocolate, Hodge says sweeter and creamier is better.
“When you add cinnamon to dark chocolate, it’s too overpowering. It becomes dry in your mouth,” he observes. “We [use] milk chocolate to bring out the cinnamon flavour but still have a nice balance of sweetness.” He says the spice also works well with white chocolate.

Hodge likes to use cinnamon in his shop’s savoury items too.
“We make a Cubano sandwich [a roast pork, ham and cheese sandwich] at Temper, and when we cook the pork in the braising liquid, we use lemons, limes and cinnamon sticks,” he explains. “The cinnamon gives a nice, earthy note to the pork, and also helps add a little bit of sweetness to the acidity of the lemons. So it has a nice after-note when you eat it – it’s not overpowering.”
At home, most people have cinnamon in powder form in their pantry, but Hodge says freshly grated cinnamon sticks have a more pungent flavour. Which you use depends on the recipe. For baked goods, the powder is best, and for soups and stocks, a whole cinnamon stick will suffice.
When it comes to Indian cuisine, Desi Indian Lounge executive chef Balvant Ajagaonkar says cinnamon is one of the five basic spices in addition to cumin, coriander, turmeric and cardamom.

“When you make basic gravies and curries, you temper or heat the oil and then add spices like cinnamon, green cardamom, cumin seeds, coriander seeds and bay leaves to release the fragrance,” explains the Vancouver-based chef. “Cinnamon has a warm and strong flavour, but you need to know how to use it and in what quantity; we use less of it relative to other spices. When we use it in curries like garam masala, we use it in a powder form, but not too much,” he cautions.
He says a cinnamon stick is usually roasted in a pan or oven with other spices before they are all ground. “In rice dishes, we add cinnamon to biryani rice and pulao,” he says. “We first temper the spices with ginger and garlic to release the flavours before adding the rice and water. If you’re using a whole cinnamon stick at the beginning, it releases the flavour slowly. But before we serve the dish, we take out the cinnamon stick because it’s not edible.”

Ajagaonkar explains that the spice is also added to chutneys like pineapple and apple, while in desserts cinnamon is in shakarpara, a dough that’s baked or deep fried. Other cinnamon-flavoured desserts include cinnamon apple raisin pudding, pistachio and cinnamon lime kulfi, and halva with apple cooked with cinnamon and other spices. Cinnamon sticks are also used to make chai.
For most Cantonese people, cinnamon is combined with star anise, cardamom and tangerine peels to make lo sui or master stock to slowly braise proteins like tofu, goose wings and eggs.
However, many Cantonese also have a negative association with cinnamon, as it is used in Chinese medicine. The bitter medicinal taste of such medicines creates a bias that Cinnabon Hong Kong director Steven Yang Tsuen-men is trying to counter.
“We [Chinese] don’t really have a sweet use of cinnamon like the Western world does,” he explains.

When he talks to potential customers who immediately dismiss cinnamon, Yang asks about their food preferences to find out where their aversion lies, and most of the time is able to persuade them to try the well-known American brand of cinnamon rolls started in Seattle, Washington, in 1985.
“I tell them that they don’t like cinnamon because it’s very strong when they [first] smell it. If you take freshly ground cinnamon, it definitely has a very sharp taste. But once you bake it with butter, margarine and frosting and everything, it’s the sweetness that comes out,” he says.

When the Cinnabon founders were developing their recipe, Yang says, they experimented with 100 different types of cinnamon and eventually settled on Makara cinnamon from Indonesia, which has a higher concentration of essential oils, giving it a more pronounced cinnamon flavour. Each tray of half a dozen Cinnabon rolls uses 20 grams of Makara cinnamon.
“They tried different recipes, but they found that this type of cinnamon has the kind of sweetness and scent that [perfectly complements] the fluffy roll with its frosting,” he says.

There are different variations of cinnamon rolls, with the oldest recorded recipe dating back to the 16th century in Germany. From there the pastry spread to Scandinavia. Europeans likely acquired cinnamon through the Silk Road via Pakistan and India.
“The cinnamon rolls are home-made – some are shaped into smaller circles, some are squarish, and some are bigger. Some are drier; some are more flaky, like a croissant,” Yang explains.
Cinnamon rolls appeared in the US thanks to German immigrants, later developing a fluffier texture and growing in size. “The European style [typically features] a glaze, whereas Cinnabon’s uses frosting, which blends well with the cinnamon and fluffy roll.”

data.title: National Games’ new generation of stars set to shine at Asian Games, Olympics and beyond
data.description:
The National Games has been more than a first for co-hosts Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau; it has also marked the emergence of the stars of the future.
And as well as the passing of the physical torch from the Greater Bay Area to Hunan, which will stage the 2029 Games, there was the passing of the metaphorical one between generations.
While the likes of Ma Long and Sun Yang, and even Pan Zhanle, grapple with the fading of their powers, those who will replace them have been stealing the limelight.
Here are some of those we think will shine at next year’s Asian Games, at the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics and beyond.
Ceci Lee Sze-wing
Where else to start but with Hong Kong’s newest sporting superstar? Cyclist Lee became the first athlete from the city to win three golds in a single Games. The road race champion in 2021, she is also alone in owning four golds overall.
The self-doubt that once plagued her, and which spoiled her first Olympics last year, has given way to an endearing assurance that translated into bold and aggressive performances on the road and track over the past fortnight.

Sarah Lee Wai-Sze, the two-time Olympic medallist, said before the Games that it was “time for a new name”. That name is Ceci Lee. She wrote in her Post diary this week that she was already focusing on next year’s Asian Games.
Beyond that, the 24-year-old will chase Olympic redemption, first in LA, then in Brisbane four years later. Improve on Sarah Lee’s bronze efforts, and Ceci Lee could lay fair claim to being the best cyclist this city has produced.
Yu Zidi
The 13-year-old swimming sensation from Hebei became Asia’s fastest ever in the 200m individual medley when she stormed to gold in two minutes and 7.41 seconds, slicing 0.16 seconds off the continental and Chinese bests of Ye Shiwen from 13 years ago.
Yu’s time in Shenzhen was one of the fastest times of the year and would have won her silver at this year’s World Aquatics Championships in Singapore and bronze at last year’s Olympics.

The teenager, who celebrated her latest birthday only last month, added 400m IM gold on the closing day of the Games meeting.
She claimed World Championships bronze this year, aged 12, in the 4x200m freestyle relay and was fourth in both the 200m IM and 200m butterfly.
Yu started swimming at six because “the summer was too hot, and my dad took me to the water park”. She has not stopped since and will be gunning for Olympic gold as a 15-year-old in 2028.
Zhang Zhanshuo
Dominant 18-year-old Zhang pieced together an astonishing Games in the pool, claiming gold in the 200m, 400m, 800m and 1,500m freestyle, along with the 4x200m freestyle relay prize.
His 400m time of 3:42.82 was a world junior record, although still more than three seconds outside this year’s world leading time of 3:39.36, established by reigning Olympic and world champion Lukas Martens.

In the 200m, Zhang’s effort would have placed him fourth in last year’s Olympic final, although he is a long way behind the world’s best over the longer distances.
Shandong’s Zhang said during the Games that he felt there remained a gap between him and the leading performers.
“I want to be closer to them … I cannot be complacent just because I’m now the national champion,” he said.
Ryan Choi Chun-yin
Fencer Choi did not manage to complete a magical 2025 quadruple after he fell in the Games’ quarter-finals, but the 28-year-old can console himself with the World Championships, Asian Championships and Shanghai Grand Prix titles he won this year.

He also got his Games golden moment after Hong Kong blitzed all comers in the team event.
In claiming his world crown, Choi managed something fellow Hongkonger and two-time Olympic champion Cheung Ka-long has not yet managed. He will defend his title on home soil next year, before switching his sights to dethroning Cheung in LA.
Zou Tianyi
While the king of foil, Hong Kong’s Cheung Ka-long, had to settle for individual National Games bronze, the 15-year-old Zou walked away with silver.
Jiangsu’s Zou gave champion Xu Jie, who had ousted Cheung, a run for his money in the final, too, mounting a comeback from 9-1 down to 9-9, before narrowly losing 15-13.
A pupil at Jiangsu Xishan Senior High School, Zou was brought back down to earth in the team semi-final, when he lost heavily to both Cheung and Ryan Choi Chun-yin.

The personal schooling in the level required to become the best of the best will do him no harm. We should see Zou back in Hong Kong for the World Championships next year.
If he carries on until the age of the oldest foil Olympic champion, Lucien Gaudin – who was 42 when he won gold in 1928 – Zou would have a whopping seven shots at Games glory through to 2052.
Xu Zhuoyi
Nicknamed “Flying Glasses” for competing while wearing spectacles, the 23-year-old 110m hurdler from Shanghai won gold in a personal best time of 13.12 seconds.
Xu’s run would have had him tied for bronze in this year’s World Athletics Championships, where he managed only 13.34 in his semi-final and was eliminated. The Chinese record still belongs to 2004 Olympic champion Liu Xiang, who ran 12.88 in 2006.

During the Paris Olympics last year, Xu won over fans at home with his “nerdy” look and became a social media sensation, with some joking they thought they had tuned in to the mathematics Olympiad when they saw him on the starting blocks.
But Xu is a serious talent, and in 2023 he claimed Asian Championships silver and Asian Games bronze. After his semi-final return in the French capital, Xu will target an improved performance in 2028.
Chen Yujie
Representing Zhejiang, the 16-year-old sprinter was crowned the National Games’ youngest women’s 100m and 200m champion. Her winning time of 11.10 seconds over the shorter distance broke the 28-year-old Asian U20 record of 11.17 seconds.
It would also have been good enough to reach the semi-final of last year’s Olympics and was the fourth-fastest U18 time in history.
Ominously for her rivals, Chen said afterwards that although she had executed her technique well, there was still plenty of scope for improvement.

After winning the 200m in 23.02 seconds, the fastest Games time since 2001, she intimated again that there was much more to come.
“I’ve been focusing more on the 100m, so my condition for the 200m wasn’t fully there,” Chen said. “The 200m result is a satisfying one for me, though it’s not my personal best.”
Chen will be fancied for more success in next year’s Asian Games, but only after sitting her school exams in January.
Liao Guifang
Weightlifter Liao won the women’s 76kg final in some style, hauling a world-record clean and jerk of 158kg on the way to adding to the world title she won in the 81kg category last year.
“I’ve been working on my technical movements and building strength, honing my skills bit by bit to develop that solid foundation,” Liao, from Fujian, said. “Only then can I perform with greater ease at competitions. My ultimate goal, of course, is the Los Angeles Olympics.”

The 24-year-old’s overall Games effort of 271kg, after she managed 113kg in the snatch, would have been sufficient for a handsome victory in last year’s Olympics.
A silver medallist in Shanxi four years ago, her career is plotting an upwards trajectory towards Los Angeles.

data.title: National Games’ lasting legacy will have an impact that transcends sport
data.description:
A colourful, energetic and innovative closing ceremony served as a fitting conclusion to the 15th National Games, which have highlighted China’s sporting excellence and showcased the Greater Bay Area. Co-hosted for the first time by Hong Kong, Macau and Guangdong, the country’s biggest multi-sport event is set to leave a lasting legacy, with an impact that transcends sport.
The Games opened on November 9 in Guangzhou and closed last night in Shenzhen, with singing, dancing, fireworks, a light show and even performing robots. Both ceremonies, with participants from Hong Kong, celebrated the culture of southern China.
Even before the opening, the sport had begun. The competition, involving more than 400 events, was fierce. World records were broken and medals shared among almost all the competing teams.
This was a breakthrough Games for Hong Kong. The city won a record haul of 19 medals, including an impressive nine golds, in swimming, cycling, fencing, sailing and rugby sevens.
Ceci Lee Sze-wing became the new queen of cycling, the first Hong Kong athlete to win three golds at the Games. Swimmer Siobhan Haughey also set a record, securing four medals, two gold and two bronze. There were many other outstanding performances, including in the triathlon and youth tennis.
The government must now, as promised, step up investment in sport, ensuring talent is nurtured, athletes well trained and a healthy sports culture established in the community. But the significance of the Games reaches beyond sporting excellence. Hong Kong successfully hosted eight sports in seven venues, accommodating thousands of visiting athletes and officials, while welcoming around 100,000 tourists. It is an experience the city can build on as it seeks to further establish itself as a leading centre for top-level sport events.
Hong Kong is only part of the equation. Staging the Games in 11 Greater Bay Area cities required close communication and cross-border connectivity between the three co-hosts, with their different systems. In the past, integration in the area has mostly taken the form of trade, investment and research. But it is clear that sport, too, can be a powerful force for development. The Games have provided a platform for further multi-sport events in the bay area, perhaps even an Olympics one day. Thomas Bach, visiting former president of the International Olympic Committee, said the bay area should “go for it,” as it had all the ingredients and could be a very important candidate.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, speaking at the closing ceremony, said the spirit of the Games would endure. The torch has been lit for the Greater Bay Area and, if the foundations laid by the Games are built on, it will continue to burn brightly.

data.title: From insults to admiration: Trump and Mamdani achieve harmony – for now
data.description:
After weeks of traded insults, New York City’s newly elected democratic socialist mayor, Zohran Mamdani, and US President Donald Trump concluded their first in-person meeting on Friday. The duo praised each other and pledged cooperation, with the “America first” leader sounding unexpectedly impressed with the young New York politician.
“Some of his ideas are the same as my ideas,” Trump said after what may ultimately go down as one of the more unexpected political encounters of the decade.
“He wants to see housing go up, see a lot of housing created, apartments built … People would be shocked, but I want to see the same thing,” he added, saying “I expect to be helping him, not hurting him”.
The 34-year-old Mamdani made history earlier this month, becoming the first democratic socialist, the first Muslim and the youngest person ever elected mayor of New York City in a victory over former New York governor Andrew Cuomo, who earned Trump’s endorsement just days before the November 4 election.
Mamdani is scheduled to succeed Democratic incumbent Eric Adams on January 1, 2026.

In the tense lead-up to the meeting, Trump spent weeks criticising Mamdani as a “Marxist” and “communist”, accusing him of wanting to remake New York in the image of “Caracas with better bagels”. Mamdani, responding on election night, told the president, “Donald Trump, since I know you’re watching … turn the volume up.”
While Trump has also called Mamdani “lunatic” and “radical”, on Friday he rejected his ally Elise Stefanik’s description of Mamdani as a “jihadist”. When asked about Mamdani calling him a “despot”, Trump brushed it off, saying: “I’ve been called much worse than a despot, so it’s not that insulting.”
“I think he’s different, and that can be in a very positive way, but I think he’s different,” Trump added.
Standing next to the president, Mamdani described their meeting as “productive” for the city they both “love”.
“We spoke about rent, we spoke about groceries, we spoke about utilities, we spoke about the different ways in which people are being pushed out,” he said.


Trump also joked with Mamdani, patting his arm as one reporter asked the mayor-elect if he thought the president was a fascist. “That’s OK, you can just say yes; it’s easier”, the president said.
Just hours before the talks, both sides struck a more measured tone. Trump told reporters before the meeting that he believed the two would “get along fine” and praised Mamdani for running a “very good race”.
Mamdani described the visit as an opportunity to underscore the needs of New York City’s 8.5 million residents and discuss federal support for housing, transit and public safety.
“I’m not concerned about this meeting. I view this meeting as an opportunity to make my case,” he said, adding, “It behoves me to leave no stone unturned in making the city more affordable.”
Born in Kampala, Uganda, to Indian filmmaker Mira Nair and scholar Mahmood Mamdani, he moved to the US as a child, grew up in Manhattan, worked as a housing counsellor and hip-hop artist, and built his profile in Queens politics before his rapid rise to the mayor’s office.

data.title: Selena Quintanilla’s iconic style through the years, as her Netflix docu Selena y Los Dinos hits screens
data.description:
It’s always a good time to celebrate the Queen of Tejano Music, Selena Quintanilla aka Selena. Netflix’s latest documentary, Selena y Los Dinos: A Family’s Legacy, does just that.


The documentary was directed by Isabel Castro, with Selena’s siblings Suzette Quintanilla and A.B. Quintanilla III serving as producers. It features never-before-seen footage of the legendary songstress from her family’s archives, making for an intimate look back at Selena – from the woman she was to the evergreen star she became.

Rather than focusing on her tragic death – the “Como La Flor” singer was murdered by the president of her fan club, Yolanda Saldívar – the Netflix title shines the light on “her life, our life and our growth”, notes Suzette, per Complex. Selena’s mother, Marcella Quintanilla, who usually keeps out of the public eye, makes an appearance as well, offering a rare glimpse into her Grammy-winning daughter’s inner world.
In the spirit of reminiscence, let’s take a look back at Selena’s distinct style.

The Selena aesthetic, which continues to influence the fashion industry today, was an inimitable blend of her Tejano roots and the dazzling glamour of stars like Janet Jackson, Whitney Houston and Madonna. She famously designed her own stage costumes with the help of Mama Quintanilla, ensuring that her sartorial choices were one of a kind.

Red lips and big hair were the signature style elements of the “Bidi Bidi Bom Bom” singer. She also loved a bedazzled bustier as much as she adored a fringed jacket.

Her leather jacket from the 1994 Tejano Music Awards was so iconic that it is now displayed at the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History.

Meanwhile, Selena’s high-waisted pants had a fandom all of their own. The star evoked effortless Western glam by styling them with buckle belts, chaps and cowboy boots.

She tapped further into classic Americana with jeans and a cropped white shirt for her performance at the 1994 Tejano Super Fest. Her staple hoop earrings perfectly complemented her features, but she’d occasionally swap them for statement pieces that matched her larger-than-life image.

When it came to dresses, Selena didn’t shy away from shine. At the 1994 Grammy Awards, where she won the trophy for Best Mexican/Mexican-American album, she donned a sparkly white Lillie Rubin number, which she bought herself at a mall in Houston, Texas.

That same year, she turned heads at the Premios Furia Musical Awards in a stunning mirror dress.

At the 1995 opening of the Hard Rock Cafe in San Antonio, Selena left our jaws hanging with a black power suit. She made sure the pinstripes sparkled.

Of course, no conversation about the star’s love for glitter would be complete without mentioning the unforgettable jumpsuit she wore for her Houston Astrodome show in 1995. Unfortunately, this would be her final televised performance.

data.title: Why China’s mega coal-fired power plants thrive amid green energy boom
data.description:
Over the Hexi Corridor, a ribbon of fertile land on the edge of the Gobi Desert blessed by the Yellow River in western China, a gleaming forest of wind turbines and solar farms stretches to the horizon.
This is Gansu, China’s renewables frontier, a major source of wind and solar energy. And yet, just beyond the dunes, another giant stirs.
At the Changle Power Plant, turbines roar to life as another 1-gigawatt coal unit comes online – six reactors now pulsing with the energy that could meet the needs of a small nation.
Here, in one of China’s greenest provinces, the future runs on two engines: one powered by the sun and wind, the other by coal.
This two-pronged strategy positions coal-fired thermal power as a safety net and grid stabiliser for intermittent renewable sources, which could protect the country against power supply crises like the one it faced in 2021.
Changle, the largest coal-fired plant in Gansu, announced in October that two more 1-gigawatt coal-fired units had successfully come into commercial operation, bringing the facility’s total capacity to 6GW.
Similar examples can be found elsewhere in China, even as the country aims to meet its twin goals of reaching peak carbon emissions by 2030 and achieving carbon neutrality by 2060.
While energy analysts are confident that China will meet the 2030 goal, some have warned that adopting a coal-focused strategy may not be environmentally sound or economically secure.


Renewable sources now account for over 60 per cent of total installed energy capacity in China, compared with around 34 per cent for coal, according to a report by the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA) and Global Energy Monitor published in August.
In June, coal’s share of China’s actual electricity generation hit a historic low of 51 per cent, down from 73 per cent in 2016.
But despite this downward trend, the nation has continued to approve new coal-fired power plants and expand existing plants.
Qi Qin, a China analyst at CREA, said there had been a surge in coal power plant approvals in 2022 and 2023, which would create more than 200GW of new capacity.
“As a result, the country is now entering a period from 2025 to 2027 when many of those projects will begin operation,” Qin said.
She said the pace of launching new coal power projects had slowed down and the country was unlikely to experience another wave of new permits.
However, in the first half of this year, new coal project proposals in the pre-permit stage reached 75GW – the highest level in a decade.
“This shows that the coal power industry is still trying to secure new projects before the expected tightening of approvals as China approaches its emissions peak,” Qin said.
President Xi Jinping announced China’s ambitious emissions targets in a speech to the UN General Assembly in 2020.
But the following year the country faced its most severe energy crisis in a decade, with more than half its provinces rationing electricity and facing power cuts.
The power supply crisis, which continued into 2022, was the result of several factors – such as rising electricity demand after the easing of the initial Covid-19 lockdowns, reduced hydropower generation due to droughts and government policies aimed at reducing carbon emissions – but coal shortages also contributed.
As a result, the authorities pledged more support for coal-fired plants to ensure a stable energy supply.


“In official rhetoric from 2022, coal power is expected to gradually take on a more flexible, supporting role: serving as a backup to variable renewables when needed,” according to the report by the CREA and Global Energy Monitor.
One of the challenges for the green energy transition is the variable power output of renewable sources – affected by factors such as the weather or time of day – with output often peaking at times when electricity demand is low.
Provinces such as southwestern Sichuan and Yunnan – which largely rely on hydropower – can also be vulnerable to environmental changes such as droughts.
In April, the National Development and Reform Commission released an implementation plan for upgrading new generation coal-fired plants up to 2027.
It said the goal for coal-fired power was to “better play its role as a safety net and support regulator in the new power system”.
It also set out strategies to build units designed to achieve reliable peak-shaving capabilities, in other words using them to meet demand at peak times and reduce the strain on the grid.
The plan also set a goal of ensuring plants had limited unplanned outages and encouraged the adoption of carbon reduction technology at coal-fired units.
“At present, China’s capacity payment policies mainly reward coal plants for being available during peak demand but offer no incentives for them to ramp down during periods of high renewable output,” Qin said.
“Instead, protective arrangements still allow coal plants to earn profits mainly through steady electricity sales, keeping them deeply embedded in the system.”
Qin said relying on coal to address intermittent renewable supplies was not economical, secure or environmentally sound for the world’s largest coal importer.
“The better solution is to enhance the flexibility of the power system and create a level playing field for all types of power generation sources to provide balancing and ancillary services, rather than protecting coal alone,” she said.
Xu Jin, chief economist at the China Energy Engineering Corporation, wrote in October that thermal power could not be replaced by new energy sources as yet.
In an article published on the social media account of China Power Enterprise Management, Xu argued that the nationwide power supply shortage in 2021 caused China to accelerate the development of coal-fired power over the course of its 14th five-year plan, which ends this year.

“These plans aimed to alleviate the power supply and demand imbalance, and support the consumption of new energy sources, completely reversing the trend of strictly controlling new coal-fired power capacity during the 13th five-year plan period,” Xu said.
“Considering the fundamental characteristics of China’s energy sector, at least until a new power system is truly established, coal-fired power will be difficult to replace with other types of power, and its dominant position in China’s power sector will be hard to shake.”
Gansu illustrates the tension between incentives for maintaining coal capacity and its role as a support for renewable integration.
By mid-October, after eight years of construction, all six coal-fired 1GW units of the Changle Power Plant were in commercial operation, according to the state-owned Gansu Electric Power Investment Group, which owns the plant.
The 6GW thermal plant will now provide power to Gansu province and boost the region’s peak-shaving capacity, as well as supplying power to surrounding areas via long-distance transmission lines.
Gansu’s grid is a major power node in northwestern China, sending energy to 25 provinces, according to China Daily.
By the end of 2024, renewables accounted for 64.4 per cent of installed capacity in Gansu, according to provincial statistics.
Last year, thermal power – primarily from coal combustion – accounted for around 47.6 per cent of actual energy generation in Gansu. Wind, hydropower and solar accounted for 20.2 per cent, 17.4 per cent and 14.8 per cent, respectively.
Qin noted that by September this year, thermal power plants made up only 27 per cent of Gansu’s installed capacity, but still generated nearly 47 per cent of the province’s electricity.
“Gansu has abundant coal reserves and a large mining industry, which makes coal a key part of the local economy,” Qin said. “Local governments and coal companies have strong incentives to keep existing coal power plants running at high output and to push for new projects.
“While these projects are often presented as necessary to ‘support renewable integration’, in practice they often serve to protect coal assets and employment.”


Despite China’s expanding coal capacity, 89 per cent of experts – surveyed by CREA and the International Society for Energy Transition Studies as part of their annual outlook report on China’s climate transition – said they were confident the country would achieve its 2030 peak carbon target.
Most of them also believed that the country could reach this peak by 2028, even though China is currently off track for its 2025 targets for carbon intensity and controlling coal consumption growth, according to the report released on November 6.
Xu wrote in his article that coal power and new energy were not “mortal enemies”, arguing that coal could provide grid stability and help alleviate operational pressures for new energy.
Xu also pointed out that China’s coal industry has faced pressure to cut emissions in recent years, including the use of carbon capture and storage technology, as well as more efficient, high-temperature coal-fired units.
Qin said coal remained an important part of China’s power system, but as renewable power generation continued to rise, its contribution would gradually decline.
The central government has also introduced a series of policies to create more space for clean power to grow.
“The main challenge, however, lies not in the direction of change but in the pace,” she said.
“Without a clear road map for phasing down coal power, the large amount of new capacity coming online in the next two years risks becoming locked into the system, slowing the clean energy transition and weakening incentives to invest in clean technology.”
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: US policeman found not guilty of murdering pregnant black woman Ta’Kiya Young
data.description:
An Ohio jury found a white police officer not guilty of murder on Friday in the 2023 fatal shooting of a pregnant black woman suspected of shoplifting from a supermarket, lawyers in the case and court records said.
Connor Grubb, an officer with the Blendon Township Police Department, was also cleared of involuntary manslaughter and felonious assault charges in the death of Ta’Kiya Young, Grubb’s lawyer Mark Collins said.
The case drew national attention because of the circumstances and has become part of a broader debate over police using excessive force against black people and other minorities.
Young, 21, died at a hospital a short time after the shooting, which took place in a community about 26km (16 miles) northeast of Columbus.
A judge had previously dropped initial charges against Grubb in the death of the unborn child, saying he could not have known she was pregnant, court records show.

“While this was the right and just outcome, it doesn’t change the fact that a young woman and her unborn baby died,” Collins said.
“We know the family of Ta’Kiya Young is grieving and their sorrow is real.”
After the verdict was read, Young’s grandmother, Nadine Young, broke down in court, according to media accounts.
“It’s not right. This is not right.”
The Young family lawyer, Sean Walton, was not immediately available for comment.
Police body camera footage of the incident showed Grubb shooting Young on August 24, 2023, in a Kroger store car park as she started to drive her car while the officer was in front of the vehicle with a gun pointed at her.
Grubb’s lawyer told the court that the officer feared for his life as the car moved forward while she ignored commands.

Erin Claypoole, a prosecuting attorney from Montgomery County who was brought in as special prosecutor in the case, told the jury earlier that Grubb knew his actions would cause harm to Young, ABC News reported.
Claypoole said that police protocols instruct officers to take reasonable steps to move out of the path of an approaching vehicle instead of discharging a firearm, the network reported.
She was not immediately available on Friday for further comment.
Grubb has been on paid administrative leave since the shooting.

data.title: Ex-Argentine leader warns Milei that cutting China ties could backfire as US push grows
data.description:
Former president Mauricio Macri warned on Friday that Argentina could weaken its export base if it distances itself from China, sharpening his public split with President Javier Milei.
Macri argued that China remains central to Argentina’s sales of soy, beef and other raw materials, goods the US already produces in abundance.
“China is more complementary than the United States for Argentina. I do not think it is good to interrupt that process,” he said.
A long-time Milei ally, Macri was instrumental in the ultra-libertarian president’s 2023 election victory.


Known as a moderating voice within the country’s right-wing faction, he is credited by diplomats and media for tempering the president’s harsh rhetoric against China after a presidential campaign where Milei called the Chinese “murderers” and promised to sever commercial ties between the two countries.
But Milei has been distancing himself from his supporter and mentor, assuming a more independent stance, especially after unexpectedly winning Argentina’s legislative elections in October, when his party captured nearly 41 per cent of the vote and 64 of 157 seats up for election.
During his interview on Friday, Macri recalled he also faced pressure from Washington during his presidency to ditch ties with Beijing, noting that Barack Obama pushed the issue politely while Donald Trump was more forceful.
Even so, he said he kept both relationships running in parallel.
“[During my presidency] I was able to tell [Trump] ‘no, we’re better friends, but I’m keeping my relationship with China,’” Macri said.
Like Milei, Macri assumed the presidency of Argentina in 2015 with a critical, sceptical stance towards China, but this changed rapidly.
During his first months, he froze construction of two hydroelectric dams in Patagonia built by the Chinese and started a review of contracts signed by the former administration with Beijing.
He also called for an investigation of Chinese activities at the deep space station in Neuquén, operated by officials of China’s People’s Liberation Army.
But by 2018, as Argentina’s finances deteriorated, Macri expanded the currency swap with China’s central bank to reinforce reserves and deepened relations with Beijing.
Later that year, he also hosted President Xi Jinping for the G20 Summit in Buenos Aires, and together they presided over the signing of dozens of cooperation agreements. At the time, he remarked that Argentina performs better “the more China develops”.
Ex-president Macri’s remarks followed Milei’s promotion of a new commercial deal with the US last week.
The Argentine administration told the media that the deal would cover tariffs, agricultural access, labour rules, environmental standards, state-run companies and digital trade – something Milei called “a tremendous piece of news”.
He also rejected claims that Washington would dominate the agreement, arguing that the United States “has many more things to put on the table” because of its economic scale.
The agreement has also been discussed amid rising friction in Washington over Argentina’s finances and export markets, with Trump officials disputing China’s influence in the South American country.
That tension surfaced publicly in late September when a photograph showed US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent reading a text message complaining that Argentina had suspended export taxes shortly after receiving a US bailout.

The tax holiday allowed Chinese buyers to snap up cheaper soybeans, prompting a rush of orders that rattled futures markets in Chicago and angered farm groups in the Midwest.
Concerns in Washington extend beyond agricultural sales. In April, Trump adviser Mauricio Claver Carone told an audience in Miami that any new IMF programme for Argentina should avoid strengthening China’s position, singling out the currency swap agreement between Beijing and Buenos Aires that he called “extortionate”, and urging the Milei government to unwind it.
Argentina renewed that swap in June last year for about US$5 billion, and economists in Buenos Aires said it remains one of the central bank’s few immediate sources of foreign currency.
